subject: Physics

topics: Activities: To measure the resistance and impedance of an inductor with or without iron core.
Activities: To measure resistance, voltage (AC/DC), current (AC) and check continuity of a given circuit using multimeter.
Activities: To assemble a household circuit comprising three bulbs, three (on/off) switches, a fuse and a power source.
Activities: To assemble the components of a given electrical circuit.
Activities: To study the variation in potential drop with length of a wire for a steady current.

Activities: To draw the diagram of a given open circuit comprising at least a battery, resistor/rheostat, key, ammeter and voltmeter. Mark the components that are not connected in proper order and correct the circuit and also the circuit diagram.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the value of v for different values of u in case of a concave mirror and to find the focal length.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the focal length of a convex mirror, using a convex lens.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the focal length of a convex lens by plotting graphs between u and v or between 1/u and 1/v .
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the focal length of a concave lens, using a convex lens.
Experiments SECTION–B: To determine angle of minimum deviation for a given prism by plotting a graph between angle of incidence and angle of deviation.
Experiments SECTION–B: To determine refractive index of a glass slab using a travelling microscope.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the refractive index of a liquid using convex lens and plane mirror.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the refractive index of a liquid using a concave mirror and a plane mirror.
Experiments SECTION–B: To draw the I-V characteristic curve for a p-n junction diode in forward and reverse bias.
Activities SECTION–B: To identify a diode, an LED, a resistor and a capacitor from a mixed collection of such items.
Activities SECTION–B: Use of multimeter to see the unidirectional flow of current in case of a diode and an LED and check whether a given electronic component (e.g., diode) is in working order.
Activities SECTION–B: To study effect of intensity of light (by varying distance of the source) on an LDR.
Activities SECTION–B: To observe refraction and lateral deviation of a beam of light incident obliquely on a glass slab.
Activities SECTION–B: To observe diffraction of light due to a thin slit.

Activities SECTION–B: To study the nature and size of the image formed by a (i) convex lens, or (ii) concave mirror, on a screen by using a candle and a screen (for different distances of the candle from the lens/mirror).
Activities SECTION–B: To obtain a lens combination with the specified focal length by using two lenses from the given set of lenses.
subtopics: Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study various factors on which the internal resistance/EMF of a cell depends.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study the variations in current flowing in a circuit containing an LDR because of a variation in (a) the power of the incandescent lamp, used to 'illuminate' the LDR (keeping all the lamps at a fixed distance). (b) the distance of an incandescent lamp (of fixed power) used to 'illuminate' the LDR.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To find the refractive indices of (a) water (b) oil (transparent) using a plane mirror, an equiconvex lens (made from a glass of known refractive index) and an adjustable object needle.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To investigate the relation between the ratio of (i) output and input voltage and (ii) number of turns in the secondary coil and primary coil of a self-designed transformer.
Suggested Investigatory Projects: To investigate the dependence of the angle of deviation on the angle of incidence using a hollow prism filled one by one, with different transparent fluids.
Suggested Investigatory Projects: To estimate the charge induced on each one of the two identical Styrofoam (or pith) balls suspended in a vertical plane by making use of Coulomb's law.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study the factor on which the self-inductance of a coil depends by observing the effect of this coil, when put in series with a resistor/(bulb) in a circuit fed up by an A.C. source of adjustable frequency.
Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study the earth's magnetic field using a compass needle -bar magnet by plotting magnetic field lines and tangent galvanometer.

topics: Record of at least 8 Experiments [with 4 from each section], to be performed by the students.
Record of at least 6 Activities [with 3 each from section A and section B], to be performed by the students.
The Report of the project carried out by the students.
Evaluation Scheme: Max. Marks: 30 Time 3 hours
Two experiments one from each section 7+7 Marks
Practical record [experiments and activities] 5 Marks
One activity from any section 3 Marks
Investigatory Project 3 Marks
Viva on experiments, activities and project 5 Marks
Total 30 marks
subtopics: Experiments SECTION–A: To determine resistivity of two / three wires by plotting a graph for potential difference versus current.
Experiments SECTION–A: To find resistance of a given wire / standard resistor using metre bridge.
Experiments SECTION–A: To verify the laws of combination (series) of resistances using a metre bridge.
Experiments SECTION–A: To verify the laws of combination (parallel) of resistances using a metre bridge.
Experiments SECTION–A: To determine resistance of a galvanometer by half-deflection method and to find its figure of merit.
Experiments SECTION–A: To convert the given galvanometer (of known resistance and figure of merit) into a voltmeter of desired range and to verify the same.
Experiments SECTION–A: To convert the given galvanometer (of known resistance and figure of merit) into an ammeter of desired range and to verify the same.
Experiments SECTION–A: To find the frequency of AC mains with a sonometer.

previousSessionContext: Chapter–1: Electric Charges and Fields: We learned that charge is discrete, conserved, and creates an invisible field around it.

sourceSessionPlan.title: Chapter–1: Electric Charges and Fields

teacherLessonNotes.sessionOverview: This session introduces the fundamental concept of electric charge, its properties (quantization and conservation), and the basic nature of electrostatic forces. Students will explore how charges interact through the lens of Coulomb's Law and the concept of an Electric Field.
prerequisiteKnowledge: Basic understanding of atomic structure (protons, electrons, neutrons)
Concept of mass and force from Class XI Physics
learningOutcomes: Define electric charge and state its two types (positive/negative).
Explain the principle of conservation of charge.
State Coulomb's Law and describe the factors affecting electrostatic force.
Define an electric field and explain it as a region of influence.

teachingPlan.teachingStrategy: Demonstration with a balloon and hair to spark curiosity about invisible forces.
Direct instruction with real-life analogies (magnets vs. charges).
Derivation on board followed by guided problem-solving.

lessonPurpose: To establish the foundation for all future topics in Electrostatics.
To connect abstract atomic concepts to observable macroscopic phenomena.
teachingSequence: Hook: Rub a balloon on hair; ask why it sticks to the wall.
Define Charge: Introduce 'q' as a property of matter.
Interaction: Explain Like charges repel, Opposite attract.
Coulomb's Law: Write formula F = k q1 q2 / r^2; explain variables.
Electric Field: Define as force per unit charge (E = F/q).
Check Understanding: Quick oral quiz on concepts.
guidedPractice: Teacher asks: 'If I double the distance, what happens to the force?' Students calculate mentally.
Teacher asks: 'Is mass conserved? Is charge conserved?' Class discusses.

lessonBlocks.title: Introduction to Charge
teacherPrompt: Have you ever felt a shock when touching a doorknob after walking on carpet?
Why does a comb rubbed with hair attract small pieces of paper?
explanation: Matter contains charged particles: Protons (+) and Electrons (-).
Charge is an intrinsic property like mass.
There are two types: Positive (deficit of electrons) and Negative (excess of electrons).
examples: Rubbing a glass rod with silk (glass becomes positive).
Rubbing a plastic rod with fur (plastic becomes negative).
boardWork: Definition: Charge is the property that causes electric force.
boardSteps: Write 'Electric Charge' in center of board.
Draw two circles with '+' and '-' signs facing each other.
solutionFlow: Identify the type of charge based on electron gain/loss.
Apply the rule: Same sign = Repulsion, Different sign = Attraction.
proofSteps: Observation: Rubbed objects attract light objects (paper).
Conclusion: A new property called 'Charge' is generated.
checkUnderstanding: If an object has excess electrons, is it positive or negative?
activity: Quick pair activity: Rub a pen on hair and bring it near paper scraps.

lessonBlocks.title: Coulomb's Law & Electric Field
teacherPrompt: How does the force change if we move the charges further apart?
What is an 'Electric Field' in simple terms?
explanation: Coulomb's Law: The force between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of their magnitudes and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
Electric Field (E): The region around a charge where it can exert force on another charge. E = F/q.
examples: Two charged balloons repelling each other.
The Earth's gravity field vs. Electric field of a point charge.
boardWork: Draw a central charge 'Q' with arrows radiating outward (Field Lines).
boardSteps: Step 1: Write F ∝ q1q2 and F ∝ 1/r^2.
Step 2: Combine to F = k q1q2 / r^2.
Step 3: Draw radial lines from a positive charge.
solutionFlow: Substitute values for q1, q2, and r.
Calculate force direction based on signs.
proofSteps: Known: Force decreases rapidly as distance increases (Inverse Square Law).
Apply: If r doubles, F becomes 1/4th.
Conclude: Distance has a huge impact on force.

geometryDiagrams.title: Electric Field Lines
geometryDiagrams.caption: Visualizing the field around a positive charge

checkUnderstanding: If the distance between two charges is halved, how does the force change?
activity: Draw field lines for a negative charge on a scrap paper.

conceptFlow.definition: Charge exists in discrete packets, not continuous amounts.
conceptFlow.coreExplanation: Total charge (q) is always an integer multiple of the elementary charge (e). Formula: q = ne, where n is an integer and e = 1.6 x 10^-19 C.
conceptFlow.importance: Explains why we cannot have half an electron's charge.
conceptFlow.whyStudyIt: Fundamental to understanding atomic interactions and current flow.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithPrevious: Connects to the existence of electrons in atoms.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithFuture: Leads to understanding electric current (flow of charge).
teacherMoves: Emphasize that n cannot be 1.5 or 2.3.
examples: A body with +2e charge has lost 2 electrons.
A body with -3e charge has gained 3 electrons.
visuals: Diagram of an atom showing discrete electron counts.
proofSteps: Fact: Electrons are indivisible in standard physics.
Apply: Charge must be a sum of whole electrons.

conceptFlow.definition: Total charge in an isolated system remains constant.
conceptFlow.coreExplanation: Charge can be transferred from one body to another, but it cannot be created or destroyed.
conceptFlow.importance: Universal law applicable to all physical processes.
conceptFlow.whyStudyIt: Critical for solving complex electrostatic problems.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithPrevious: Follows the concept of charge transfer.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithFuture: Basis for Kirchhoff's laws in circuits.
teacherMoves: Use the analogy of money: You can move money between accounts, but total money stays same.
examples: Glass rod (+) and silk (-): Total charge before = 0, after = (+q) + (-q) = 0.
visuals: Two spheres touching and separating with equal and opposite charges.
proofSteps: Observation: Rubbing creates +q on one, -q on other.

classroomQuestions.question: Can a body have a charge of 0.5e?
What happens to the force if we double both charges and keep distance same?
answerPoints: F is proportional to product of charges.

commonMisconceptionsDetailed.misconception: Charge is created when we rub objects.
commonMisconceptionsDetailed.correction: Charge is only transferred; electrons move from one object to another. Total charge remains zero.

commonMisconceptionsDetailed.misconception: Electric field lines are physical wires.
commonMisconceptionsDetailed.correction: They are imaginary lines representing the direction and strength of the force.

slowLearners: Use visual analogies (magnets) for charge interaction.
Focus on qualitative understanding before formulas.
averageLearners: Practice simple numerical problems using q=ne.
Draw field lines for single charges.
advancedLearners: Discuss the vector nature of Coulomb's force.
Predict field patterns for two opposite charges.

teacherTips: Always emphasize that 'like' charges repel and 'unlike' attract.
Use a whiteboard marker to draw field lines dynamically.
Relate the concept of 'field' to gravity which students already know.
misconceptions: Charge is a fluid that flows out.
formativeChecks: Ask students to hold up fingers: 1 for repel, 2 for attract when describing charge pairs.
assessmentQuestions: State the principle of conservation of charge with an example.
Calculate the number of electrons in a charge of -1 Coulomb.

timePlan.purpose: Refresh atomic structure and spark curiosity.
Teach Charge properties and Coulomb's Law.

sessionSummary: We learned that charge is quantized, conserved, and creates an electric field.
nextSessionBridge: Today we saw how charges interact. Next, we will study how these charges behave in different materials (Conductors vs Insulators) and the concept of Electric Flux.

learningOutcomes: Students will define electric charge and distinguish between positive and negative types.
Students will explain the quantization of charge using q=ne.
Students will state and apply Coulomb's Law to calculate electrostatic force.
Students will define an electric field and describe its direction.
sourceSessionPlan.introduction: Imagine you are walking across a carpet in your socks and then touch a metal door handle. *ZAP!* You feel a shock. Why? Where did that energy come from? Today, we aren't just talking about shocks; we are going to uncover the invisible force that makes magnets stick to fridges, lightning strike the ground, and your hair stand up when you comb it. This invisible force is called 'Electric Charge'.

theory.overview: Electric charge is a fundamental property of matter that causes it to experience a force when placed in an electromagnetic field. There are two types: positive (protons) and negative (electrons). Charges interact via electrostatic forces described by Coulomb's Law, and they create an electric field around them.
keyPoints: Total charge in an isolated system is conserved.
Like charges repel; opposite charges attract.
Coulomb's Law defines the force between point charges.
theory.detailedContent: Electric charge is not a new substance but a property of matter, similar to mass. Just as mass attracts other masses via gravity, charge interacts with other charges via electrostatic force. The magnitude of this force is governed by Coulomb's Law: F = k(q1q2)/r^2. This means the force increases if charges get bigger and decreases rapidly if they move apart (inverse square law). Furthermore, charge cannot be created or destroyed; it can only be transferred from one body to another (Conservation of Charge). Also, charge comes in discrete packets called electrons; you cannot have half an electron's worth of charge (Quantization). The region around a charge where it exerts force on another charge is called the Electric Field.

activities.name: The Balloon Stick Challenge
instructions: Step 1: Students rub a balloon on their hair or woolen sweater for 10 seconds.
Step 2: Bring the balloon close to a wall (without touching).
Step 3: Observe if it sticks and discuss why (induced charge attraction).
Step 4: Try to stick two rubbed balloons together. Do they attract or repel?

activities.name: Human Field Lines
instructions: Step 1: Select one student to be a 'Positive Charge' standing in the center.
Step 2: Other students act as 'Test Charges' and must move away from the center (repulsion).
Step 3: Draw imaginary lines on the floor showing the path they take.
Step 4: Discuss how the 'force' gets weaker as they move further away.

studentLessonNotes.title: Electric Charges and Fields: The Invisible Force
studentLessonNotes.sessionOverview: Discover how electric charges create invisible fields, learn the rules of attraction and repulsion, and understand the fundamental properties of charge.
studentLessonNotes.introduction: Welcome to Chapter 1! Today, we are going to unlock the secrets of electricity. You already know that static electricity makes your hair stand up or a balloon sticks to a wall. But have you ever wondered *why*? In this session, we will explore the invisible world of electric charges and the powerful fields they create around them.
learningObjectives: Explain the fundamental properties of electric charge (quantization and conservation).
Distinguish between conductors and insulators based on charge movement.
Describe how an electric field is created by a point charge.
Apply Coulomb's Law to calculate the force between two charges.
quickRecall: Matter is made of atoms containing protons (+) and electrons (-).
Like charges repel; opposite charges attract.
Charge cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred.
easyToRemember: Charge comes in packets (quanta), never in fractions.
Total charge before = Total charge after.

comparisonTables.title: Conductors vs. Insulators
rows: Free to move (e.g., electrons flow easily)

formulaCards.title: Quantization of Charge
formulaCards.meaning: Total charge (q) is an integer multiple of the elementary charge (e).
formulaCards.whenToUse: When calculating total charge on a body or finding the number of electrons.

formulaCards.title: Coulomb's Law
formulaCards.meaning: The force between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of their magnitudes and inversely proportional to the square of the distance.
formulaCards.whenToUse: To calculate the electrostatic force between two stationary point charges.

geometryDiagrams.title: Electric Field Lines of a Positive Charge
geometryDiagrams.caption: Radial lines pointing outward from a positive point charge.

proofSteps: Charge is quantized because electrons are discrete particles.
Total charge in an isolated system remains constant (Conservation Law).
Force decreases rapidly as distance increases (Inverse Square Law).

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking charge can be 1.5e or any fraction.
commonMistakes.correction: Charge is always an integer multiple of e (q = ne).

commonMistakes.mistake: Confusing the direction of force for like charges.
commonMistakes.correction: Like charges repel (force pushes away); opposites attract (force pulls together).
commonMistakes.example: Two positive charges push each other apart.

sections.heading: The Nature of Electric Charge
sections.explanation: Electric charge is a fundamental property of matter that causes it to experience a force when placed in an electromagnetic field. There are two types: positive (protons) and negative (electrons).
keyPoints: Charge is scalar (has magnitude, no direction).
Like charges repel; opposite charges attract.
examples: Rubbing a glass rod with silk gives it positive charge.
Rubbing a plastic rod with wool gives it negative charge.
sections.whyItMatters: Understanding charge is the foundation for all electricity, from lightning to your smartphone battery.
sections.detailedExplanation: The smallest unit of charge is the elementary charge (e), carried by an electron or proton. Any object's total charge (q) must be a whole number multiple of this basic unit: q = ne, where n is an integer (positive, negative, or zero). This means you can never have a fraction of an electron's charge.
observedIn: Hair standing up after removing a hat
visualSupport: Diagram showing electrons moving from wool to plastic rod.

visualAssets.prompt: A simple illustration showing a plastic rod and a piece of wool. Arrows indicate electrons moving from the wool to the rod, making the rod negative and the wool positive.
visualAssets.alt: Electrons transferring from wool to plastic rod

importantNotes: The total charge of an isolated system never changes.
memoryTechniques: Think of charge like money: you can have $1, $2, but not $1.50 in whole coins.
conceptSummary: Charge comes in discrete packets and is never created or destroyed.

sections.heading: Coulomb's Law: The Force Between Charges
sections.explanation: This law tells us exactly how strong the push or pull is between two charged objects. It depends on how much charge they have and how far apart they are.
keyPoints: Force is directly proportional to the product of charges.
Force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance.
Force acts along the line joining the centers.
examples: Doubling the distance reduces the force to 1/4th.
Doubling one charge doubles the force.
sections.whyItMatters: This law is the 'gravity' of electricity. It explains why atoms hold together and why lightning jumps across the sky.
sections.detailedExplanation: Coulomb's Law states that the force (F) between two point charges is given by F = k * |q1 * q2| / r^2. Here, 'k' is a constant, 'q1' and 'q2' are the magnitudes of the charges, and 'r' is the distance between them. Notice the 'r squared' in the denominator: if you move the charges twice as far apart, the force becomes four times weaker!
observedIn: Repulsion between two similarly charged balloons
Attraction between a charged comb and paper bits
visualSupport: Diagram showing two charges with arrows indicating force direction and length representing magnitude.

visualAssets.prompt: Two spheres labeled +q1 and +q2. Arrows point away from each other (repulsion). The arrow length is short because they are far apart.
visualAssets.alt: Repulsive force between two positive charges

importantNotes: Always use SI units (Coulombs for charge, meters for distance).
The force is mutual: Charge A pulls B just as hard as B pulls A.
conceptSummary: Force grows with charge size but shrinks rapidly with distance.

sections.heading: Electric Fields: The Invisible Reach
sections.explanation: A charge doesn't just sit there; it changes the space around it. This modified space is called an electric field. Any other charge entering this space feels a force.
keyPoints: Field lines originate from positive charges and end at negative charges.
Field lines never cross each other.
The density of lines indicates field strength.
examples: Compass needles aligning in a magnetic field (analogy).
Paper bits jumping toward a charged rod.
sections.whyItMatters: Fields allow us to understand how forces act at a distance without physical contact. It's the mechanism of all wireless energy transfer.
sections.detailedExplanation: Imagine a positive charge as a sun and the electric field as its gravity. The lines radiate outward from the positive charge and curve inward toward negative charges. If you draw two lines, they will never cross because a single point in space cannot have two different force directions at once.
observedIn: Dust particles clustering around a TV screen
The 'hair standing up' effect near high voltage wires
visualSupport: Diagram of radial lines coming out of a positive charge and going into a negative charge.

visualAssets.prompt: A central positive charge with arrows radiating outward in all directions. A separate negative charge with arrows pointing inward.
visualAssets.alt: Electric field lines around positive and negative charges

importantNotes: Field lines are imaginary but represent real physical effects.
Inside a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium, the electric field is zero.
conceptSummary: Electric fields map out the force a charge would feel at any point in space.

definitions.definition: The principle that electric charge exists only in discrete packets, where total charge q is an integer multiple of the elementary charge e (q = ne).
The region around a charged particle where it exerts an electrostatic force on other charged particles.
The total electric charge in an isolated system remains constant over time; it can be transferred but not created or destroyed.

workedExamples.title: Calculating Force Between Two Charges
workedExamples.problem: Two point charges, q1 = 2 μC and q2 = -3 μC, are placed 0.5 meters apart in air. Calculate the magnitude of the force between them.
steps: Identify the values and convert to SI units.
Substitute values into Coulomb's Law formula.
reasoning: We use the magnitude of charges because we are calculating force size.
The negative sign indicates attraction, but the magnitude is positive.
workedExamples.explanation: First, we convert microcoulombs to Coulombs. Then we plug everything into the formula. Since the charges have opposite signs, they attract each other with a force of 0.216 Newtons.

definitions: Electric field lines start at + and end at -.
quickRecap: Force drops by 4x if distance doubles.

selfCheckQuestions: If I double the charge on one object and halve the distance, how does the force change?
Can an object have a charge of 1.5e? Why or why not?
Draw the field lines for two positive charges placed near each other.
quickSummary: Electric charge is discrete and conserved.
Coulomb's Law governs the force between charges.
Electric fields visualize the influence of charges in space.

fillInTheBlanks.prompt: The smallest unit of electric charge is called the ________.
Electric field lines never ________ each other.

mcqQuestions.question: Which of the following is true regarding electric charge?
options: A. Charge can be created but not destroyed
D. Charge depends on mass only

mcqQuestions.question: If the distance between two charges is doubled, the force becomes:

veryShortAnswerQuestions.question: State the principle of conservation of charge.
veryShortAnswerQuestions.answer: The total electric charge in an isolated system remains constant.

veryShortAnswerQuestions.question: What is the value of elementary charge?

didYouKnow: The total positive charge in a proton is exactly equal to the total negative charge in an electron, making atoms electrically neutral.
summary: We learned that charge comes in packets (quantization) and follows conservation laws. We also explored how charges create invisible fields and exert forces described by Coulomb's Law.

homework.title: Quantization of Charge Calculation
homework.instructions: Calculate the total charge on an object that has gained or lost a specific number of electrons. Use the elementary charge value.
homework.question: A plastic rod is rubbed with wool and acquires a net charge of -4.8 x 10^-9 C. (a) How many electrons were transferred to the rod? (b) If the rod was initially neutral, what is the mass of the electrons transferred? (Take mass of electron = 9.1 x 10^-31 kg and magnitude of charge on electron = 1.6 x 10^-19 C).
homework.answerSpace: Show all steps of calculation clearly.
homework.visualRequirement: Draw a simple diagram showing the rod with negative charges distributed on its surface.
homework.expectedResponse: (a) n = Q/e = 30 electrons. (b) Mass = 30 * 9.1 x 10^-31 kg.

homework.title: Properties of Electric Charge
homework.instructions: Select the correct option that best describes the fundamental properties of electric charge discussed in class.
homework.question: Which of the following statements is NOT true regarding electric charge?
options: Charge is quantized and exists in discrete packets.
Total charge in an isolated system remains constant.
Charge can be created or destroyed independently of matter.
Like charges repel and unlike charges attract.
homework.answerSpace: Write the letter of the correct option.

homework.title: Visualizing Electric Fields
homework.instructions: Draw the electric field lines for a single positive point charge and a single negative point charge. Label the direction of the field vectors.
homework.question: Sketch the electric field pattern around: (a) An isolated positive charge (+q). (b) An isolated negative charge (-q). Indicate the direction of the field lines with arrows.
homework.answerSpace: Draw on a separate sheet of paper.
homework.visualRequirement: The diagram must show radial lines. For positive charge, arrows point outward. For negative charge, arrows point inward.
homework.expectedResponse: Correct representation of radial field lines with appropriate arrow directions.

homework.title: Charge in Daily Life
homework.instructions: Explain a common phenomenon using the concept of charge conservation and discrete nature.
homework.question: When you comb your dry hair, the comb attracts small pieces of paper. Explain this phenomenon in terms of charge transfer and conservation. Why does the paper get attracted even if it is neutral?
homework.expectedResponse: Explanation involving electron transfer from hair to comb (or vice versa), creation of an electric field, and induction causing attraction in neutral paper.

homework.title: Conceptual Reasoning on Fields
homework.instructions: Analyze the concept of an 'invisible field' mentioned in class.
homework.question: The session stated that charge creates an 'invisible field'. If you place a test charge at a point where no other charges are visible nearby, how do you know if an electric field exists there? Describe the method to detect it.
homework.expectedResponse: By placing a small positive test charge at that point and observing if it experiences an electrostatic force. If a force is felt, a field exists.

homework.title: Observing Static Electricity
homework.instructions: Perform a safe home activity to observe charge transfer.
homework.question: Rub a plastic pen or comb with dry wool or hair. Bring it near small pieces of paper. Observe the interaction. Record your observation and explain which property of charge (discrete/conserved) is demonstrated by the fact that the paper moves towards the comb.
homework.answerSpace: Write down what happened and your explanation.
homework.expectedResponse: Observation: Paper jumps to comb. Explanation: Charge transfer occurred (conservation), creating a field that acts on the paper.

learningOutcomesCovered: LO1: Understand that charge is discrete and conserved.
LO2: Understand that charge creates an invisible field.
